name: Sierra de Aracena Villages Circuit
name_original: Cortelazor la Real, Cañaveral de León, Los Marines – circular por Sierra de Aracena y Picos de Aroche
description: Cycle a difficult 38.7-mile route through Sierra de Aracena y Picos de Aroche, connecting Cortelazor la Real, Cañaveral de León, and Los Mar
region_intro: Road cycling around Cortelazor, nestled within the Sierra de Aracena y Picos de Aroche Natural Park, offers diverse terrain for cyclists. The region features rolling hills and mountains, providing both challenging climbs and rewarding descents. Cyclists will encounter lush oak and chestnut forests, along with streams and natural rock formations. Panoramic vistas are common, especially from mountain passes like Alto del Palancar. Best road cycling routes around Cortelazor The most popular road cycling route is Sierra de Aracena Villages Circuit, a 38.7 miles (62.2 km) trail that takes 3 hours 19 minutes to complete. This difficult route connects several traditional villages within the natural park. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is Sierra de Aracena White Villages Loop, a difficult 37.0 miles (59.5 km) path. This route offers varied terrain and passes through picturesque whitewashed villages. Local road cyclists also love the Fuenteheridos, Cortegana, Almonaster la Real, La Peña de Arias Montano – circular route, a 29.2 miles (47.0 km) trail leading through wooded hills, often completed in about 2 hours 36 minutes. Road cycling around Cortelazor is defined by rolling hills, oak and chestnut forests, and panoramic vistas. The extensive network of roads offers options for different ability levels, from moderate to difficult. The routes in Cortelazor are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 5.0 stars from over 4 reviews. More than 300 road cyclists have used komoot to explore Cortelazor's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: What is the typical terrain like for road cycling near Cortelazor?
A: The region around Cortelazor, nestled in the Sierra de Aracena y Picos de Aroche Natural Park, is characterized by rolling hills and mountains. You'll encounter challenging climbs and rewarding descents, often through lush oak and chestnut forests. Panoramic vistas are common, especially from mountain passes like Alto del Palancar, offering sweeping views of the valleys below.

Q: Are there road cycling routes suitable for beginners around Cortelazor?
A: While many routes in the area involve significant elevation gains, there are options for various fitness levels. The guide data indicates 13 moderate routes out of a total of 35, offering a less strenuous experience compared to the more difficult trails. However, true beginner routes (classified as 'easy') are not specifically listed for road cycling in this area.

Q: What kind of natural features can I expect to see on road cycling routes in Cortelazor?
A: Road cycling routes often wind through the unique *dehesa* ecosystem, dominated by holm oaks, cork oaks, and chestnut trees. You might also discover hidden streams and natural rock formations sculpted by erosion. For a specific natural highlight, consider visiting the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/7287170" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Waterfall in the Sierra de Aracena and Picos de Aroche Natural Park&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: Are there any long-distance road cycling routes available?
A: Yes, Cortelazor is part of an extensive network of roads that connect charming villages. For a longer ride, consider the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e991335581/sierra-de-aracena-and-picos-de-aroche-park-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Sierra de Aracena and Picos de Aroche Park Loop&lt;/a&gt;, which covers approximately 78.7 km and features significant elevation gain, making it a challenging and rewarding option.

Q: What cultural attractions or villages can I visit along the road cycling routes?
A: Many road cycling routes pass through or connect with quaint whitewashed villages, offering opportunities to experience local culture and history. Cortelazor itself has an old center declared of Cultural Interest. Nearby, you can explore &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4090741" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Los Marines Village&lt;/a&gt; or the historic &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5301554" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Castaño del Robledo&lt;/a&gt;. Routes like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e991720439/sierra-de-aracena-villages-circuit" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Sierra de Aracena Villages Circuit&lt;/a&gt; are designed to connect several traditional villages.

Q: Are there any circular road cycling routes around Cortelazor?
A: Yes, there are several excellent circular road cycling routes. Popular options include the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e991720439/sierra-de-aracena-villages-circuit" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Sierra de Aracena Villages Circuit&lt;/a&gt;, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e991715501/sierra-de-aracena-white-villages-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Sierra de Aracena White Villages Loop&lt;/a&gt;, and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e991719809/fuenteheridos-cortegana-almonaster-la-real-la-pena-de-arias-montano-circular-route" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Fuenteheridos, Cortegana, Almonaster la Real, La Peña de Arias Montano – circular route&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: What are some notable landmarks or viewpoints along the road cycling routes?
A: You'll find several points of interest offering stunning views. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4184233" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Arias Montano Peak and the Hermitage of Our Lady of the Angels&lt;/a&gt; provides panoramic vistas. Another great spot for views is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2894628" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Alájar Viewpoint&lt;/a&gt;. Additionally, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5300344" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Aracena Castle&lt;/a&gt; offers historical significance and panoramic views.

Q: Can I find places for coffee or tapas along the road cycling routes?
A: Absolutely. The routes often pass through quaint villages where you'll find opportunities for coffee stops or to enjoy local tapas. These villages are an integral part of the cycling experience in the Sierra de Aracena, allowing you to refuel and immerse yourself in the local culture.

Q: What do other road cyclists enjoy most about road cycling in Cortelazor?
A: The area is highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 5.0 stars from over 4 reviews. Cyclists often praise the combination of challenging yet scenic routes, the beautiful forested landscapes, and the charm of traditional Andalusian villages. Over 300 road cyclists have used komoot to explore Cortelazor's varied terrain.

Q: What is the best season for road biking in Cortelazor?
A: Given the region's climate, spring and autumn generally offer the most pleasant conditions for road cycling, with milder temperatures and lush landscapes. Summers can be quite hot in Andalusia, making early morning or late afternoon rides preferable. Winters are typically mild, allowing for year-round cycling, though some days might be cooler or wetter.

Q: Are there any challenging road cycling routes for experienced riders?
A: Yes, the region is well-suited for experienced riders seeking a challenge. Many routes feature significant climbs, often involving elevations of 1000-1500m. For instance, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e991720439/sierra-de-aracena-villages-circuit" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Sierra de Aracena Villages Circuit&lt;/a&gt; is classified as difficult, with over 1100 meters of elevation gain over 62 km.

Q: How many road cycling routes are available around Cortelazor?
A: There are over 30 road cycling routes available around Cortelazor, offering a diverse range of experiences. These routes vary in difficulty, with 13 classified as moderate and 22 as difficult, catering to different skill levels.
tips: Magnificent viewpoint on the local road, which connects the Huelva town of Cañaveral de León with the nearby Fuentes de León. It has benches, litter bins and railings, from which to observe the valley where Cañaveral de León and the Sierra that are in front of it sits. Starlight Zone to see the stars, due to its low light pollution. And it is also an area where you can see vultures that come to the area to gain height with the ascending thermal currents.

The Cañaveral de León lagoon is a pool that collects groundwater from the Fuente Redonda spring and serves to irrigate the crops in the area. For a few years it has also been conditioned to be the municipal swimming pool of this town. In summer a treatment plant is started up to offer a pleasant bath on the hottest days.

The town of Aracena is dominated by its castle, where it is located on a hill and where you can see the group of houses and surroundings that surround it. The Castle of Aracena is accompanied by the Iglesia del Castillo.

The name of Los Marines has a curious history: during a visit by King Felipe II to the municipality, an inhabitant asked him why, being so powerful, he did not bring the sea to the town. The king's response was: "I will not bring the sea, but you will be sailors." Since then, all the young people of the town performed their military service in Marina Huelva.
This picturesque municipality, surrounded by nature and beauty, is an ideal place to explore during a getaway to the Sierra de Huelva. Don't hesitate to visit Los Marines and enjoy its charm.

The town of Cortelacía is home to the Parroquia Nuestra Señora de Los Remedios, which is located in the main square where the town hall is also located. The center is in the old middle of the Picos de Aroche.

faqs.question: What is the difficulty level and terrain like for this road cycling route?
faqs.answer: This circular route is rated as difficult and requires a very good fitness level. While primarily paved, the terrain is notably hilly with significant ascents, including a segment on the Cortelazor-Los Marines climb that features a maximum gradient of 19.1%. Expect a challenging ride through the Sierra de Aracena.

faqs.question: How long does it typically take to complete this circular road cycling route?
faqs.answer: The route is estimated to take approximately 3 hours and 20 minutes to complete. However, this duration can vary depending on your cycling pace, fitness level, and how often you stop to enjoy the scenery or visit villages.

faqs.question: What are some notable sights or landmarks along the route?
faqs.answer: You'll encounter several interesting points, including the El Puerto Viewpoint, offering panoramic views. The route also passes by the Laguna de Cañaveral de León, and you'll be near the historic Aracena Castle. The charming Los Marines Village and the Parish of Our Lady of Remedies in Cortelazor are also along the way.

faqs.question: Where can I park when starting this cycling route?
faqs.answer: While specific parking areas for this exact route are not detailed, you can typically find parking in the villages along the route, such as Cortelazor la Real, Cañaveral de León, or Los Marines. Look for public parking spaces within these towns to begin your ride.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: The Sierra de Aracena y Picos de Aroche benefits from a mild climate and high rainfall, making it lush. Spring and autumn are generally ideal for cycling, offering pleasant temperatures and vibrant landscapes. Summers can be hot, and winters, while mild, might bring more rain.

faqs.question: Is this road cycling route dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: As this is a road cycling route, it's generally not recommended for dogs to accompany you, especially given the challenging terrain and potential for traffic. For dog-friendly activities, consider hiking trails within the natural park where dogs on a leash are often permitted, but always check specific regulations.

faqs.question: Are there any permits or fees required to cycle within the Sierra de Aracena y Picos de Aroche Natural Park?
faqs.answer: Generally, there are no specific permits or entrance fees required for road cycling on public roads within the Parque Natural de Sierra de Aracena y Picos de Aroche. However, always respect local regulations and private property. For more detailed information, you can consult official tourism sites like visit-andalucia.com.

faqs.question: Does this route intersect with other well-known trails?
faqs.answer: Yes, this route partially overlaps with several other trails. You'll find sections that are part of the [CIMA HL06] Sierra del Viento * Rivera de Hinojales, [CIMA HL03] Aracena, and the Camino de Santiago del Sur, among others. This offers opportunities to explore different paths if you wish to extend or modify your ride.

faqs.question: Are there places to eat or get refreshments along the way?
faqs.answer: Yes, the route passes through several charming villages like Cortelazor la Real, Cañaveral de León, and Los Marines. These villages typically offer local cafes, bars, and restaurants where you can stop for refreshments, a meal, or to sample local specialties like the famous 'jamón Ibérico'.

faqs.question: What kind of natural environment can I expect to see while cycling?
faqs.answer: You'll cycle through the stunning Sierra de Aracena y Picos de Aroche Natural Park, known for its extensive forests, wooded valleys, and picturesque white villages. The landscape is rich with holm oak, cork oak, olive trees, and large masses of chestnut trees. Keep an eye out for the famous black Iberian pigs foraging under the holm oaks!

faqs.question: Is this route suitable for beginner road cyclists or families?
faqs.answer: Given its 'difficult' rating, significant elevation gain, and steep gradients, this route is not suitable for beginner road cyclists or families with young children. It requires a very good fitness level and experience with challenging climbs. Less experienced cyclists should look for flatter, shorter routes in the region.

waypoints.name: Parish of Our Lady of Remedies, Cortelazor
